title: Soupe au pistou
subtitle: Provençal summer vegetable soup, finished with a basil-and-garlic pistou.
excerpt: A summer vegetable soup from Provence: white beans, courgette, green beans, tomato, and small pasta in a light broth, then a spoonful of pistou stirred in at the table. The pistou (raw basil, garlic, olive oil, and parmesan, pounded in a mortar) is what makes the bowl.
sourceNotes: Soupe au pistou is the Provençal cousin of the Ligurian minestrone alla genovese; both descend from a shared coastal tradition of summer vegetable soups finished with a raw herb paste. The Provençal version uses no pine nuts in the pistou (that is the Genoese pesto) and traditionally no cheese, though parmesan is now widely added. The earliest written codifications appear in late nineteenth-century Provençal cookery books; the dish is older. Mrs Beeton (Book of Household Management, 1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) describes vegetable potages of the same shape in her French chapter.

recipe.freezeNotes: The soup base (without pasta or pistou) freezes well for up to three months. Make the pistou fresh on the day; it loses its colour in a day in the fridge and most of its character in the freezer.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. The pistou is the rate-limiting step: pounding 12 servings' worth by hand is slow. A small food processor works if scale demands it.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Soup base improves overnight. Cook the pasta on the day and add it at the end so it does not turn to mush.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Provençal raw paste of basil, garlic, olive oil, and (modern usage) parmesan, pounded in a pestle and mortar. Distinguished from Ligurian pesto by the absence of pine nuts; Provençal traditionalists also omit the cheese.

content.text: Two pots of work for a clean bowl of summer. The vegetables simmer with white beans and a little pasta until the broth has taken their flavour. Separately, basil leaves and garlic are pounded with olive oil and parmesan into a green, gluey paste. The paste goes in at the table, a heaped spoon per bowl, and is stirred through just before the first mouthful.

has no pine nuts (that is Ligurian pesto) and historically no cheese; modern Provençal recipes have added parmesan and we follow that here. The other difference is that the paste is never cooked. The heat of the soup is enough to release the oils from the basil; cooking the basil dulls it to grass.

items.prepNote: small shape: ditalini, broken vermicelli, or orzo

content.text: Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} chopped onion with a pinch of salt and cook for five minutes until soft but not coloured. Add the {{carrot}} diced carrots and cook for three minutes more. Tip in the {{green-beans}} of green beans, the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tinned tomatoes, and the {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring up to a simmer and cook for fifteen minutes.
Add the courgette, beans, and pasta

Add the {{courgette}} diced courgette, the {{cannellini-beans}} of drained cannellini beans, and the {{pasta-dried}} of pasta. Cook for ten minutes more, or until the pasta is just tender. Taste and adjust salt. The broth should taste of vegetables, lightly seasoned; the pistou will bring most of the flavour.

Crush the {{garlic}} of garlic with a pinch of salt in a pestle and mortar until smooth. Add the {{basil}} of basil leaves a handful at a time, pounding and grinding until the leaves break down into a rough paste. Trickle in the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil while stirring with the pestle. Stir in the {{parmesan}} of grated parmesan last. The paste should be thick, bright green, and smell intensely of basil. If pounding by hand is impractical, a small food processor with short pulses works; never blend smooth or the basil oxidises grey.

Ladle the soup into warm bowls. Add a heaped tablespoon of pistou to each bowl at the table and let each person stir it in themselves; the smell hitting the hot soup is part of the pleasure. Pass the rest of the pistou separately.

items.cause: Basil was over-processed, or the food processor heated the leaves.
items.fix: Use shorter pulses, or switch to pestle and mortar. Add the cheese only at the end after the basil has been knocked back with oil.

items.symptom: Pistou turns dull green, almost grey
items.cause: Air exposure or heat; or the basil was bruised before pounding.
items.fix: Cover the surface of the pistou with a thin film of olive oil if making ahead. It will hold colour for an hour or two; longer than that it loses brightness.

items.cause: Under-seasoned, or vegetable stock too light.
items.fix: The pistou supplies a lot of the seasoning. Taste with the pistou stirred in before adjusting; you may need only a turn of pepper.

items.symptom: Pasta has gone to mush by the second bowl
items.cause: Pasta cooked in the soup and continued to absorb broth on standing.
items.fix: Cook the pasta separately and add to each bowl just before serving. Or hold the soup off the heat and reheat for each portion.

content.text: Borlotti or haricot beans work in place of cannellini. Add a piece of parmesan rind to the broth as it simmers and lift it out at the end. For a heavier bowl, drop the pasta and double the white beans. Vegan: omit the parmesan, double the olive oil in the pistou, and add a tablespoon of nutritional yeast.

The dish is summer's bowl in Provence: made when the courgettes and beans are at their best and the basil is heavy on the plant. It crosses the Italian border into Liguria as minestrone alla genovese, with pine nuts in the herb paste and a different mix of vegetables. Either side of the border, the principle is the same: vegetables cooked just enough to share flavour, finished with raw herbs that have not been near a pan.
